coredata.dc:title: Ultra-stable carbon quantum dot nanofluids for direct absorption solar collectors
coredata.prism:publicationName: Solar Energy Materials and Solar Cells
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Xingyu
Xiong, Zhekun
Chen, Meijie
Zhou, Ping
coredata.dc:description: Nanofluids show great interest in the direct absorption solar collector (DASC) due to their excellent optical, thermal, and flow properties. However, the stability of nanofluid in the terms of the long period and the working temperature has limited its commercial application in DASCs. In this work, ultra-stable carbon quantum dot (CQD) nanofluids were prepared by microwave heating polyethylene glycol 200 (PEG-200), which shows excellent stability over a long period (such as 30 days) and repeatable performance at the working temperature. The solar thermal conversion efficiency η increases with extending the microwave duration t, and η can reach 81% at 1 sun and t = 24 min, which is triple the base fluid PEG-200 (η = 27%). In addition, the solar intensity and working temperature have little effect on η due to the ultra-stability of CQD nanofluids. Finally, η can be further enhanced by changing the base fluid or mixing with the plasmonic nanofluids. These results show that the CQD nanofluids can be a potential working fluid used in solar thermal applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Solar thermal
Carbon quantum dot
Stability
Nanofluid

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dot nanofluid
Solar-thermal conversion performances of CQD nanofluids
Enhanced solar-thermal conversion performances of CQD nanofluids

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic diagram of the synthesis process of carbon quantum dot nanofluid; (b) Schematic diagram of the potential process of CQD formation based on PEG. Revised with permission from Ref. [28] Copyrights 2014, Royal Society of Chemistry.
Schematic diagram of the photothermal conversion experimental setup (Numbers 1–5 indicate simulated solar light, stirring bar, quartz container, working fluid, and sponge plate respectively).

(a)–(c) Effect of microwave radiation duration on the temperature of the sample and its appearance (radiation power of 700 W): (a) Infrared thermogram (radiation duration of 3 min); (b) Temperature distribution; (c) Optical images; (d) TEM characterization of CQD nanofluids with different magnifications (radiation duration of 24 min); (e) Effect of microwave radiation duration on the spectral properties of samples.

Stability of CQD nanofluids over a long period: (a) Optical images of CQD nanofluids (from left to right: microwave radiation duration range from 3 to 24 min with an interval of 3 min) before and after 30-day storage; (b) Transmission spectra of CQD nanofluids with microwave radiation duration of 24 min after storage for different days.
Temperature variation curves of CQD nanofluid (radiation duration is 24 min) at different horizontal positions under 1 sun irradiation: (a) without stirring, (b) with stirring at 500 rpm.

(a) Temperature variation curves of CQD nanofluid with radiation duration of 0min, 3min, 12min and 24min at 1 sun illumination intensity; (b) to (e) Solar-thermal conversion efficiency of CQD nanofluid with different microwave radiation durations at 1 sun illumination intensity: (b) 0 min; (c) 3 min; (d) 12 min; (e) 24 min.

(a) Temperature variation curves of CQD nanofluid (radiation duration of 24 min) under different light intensities; (b) to (e) Solar -thermal conversion efficiency of CQD nanofluid (radiation duration of 24 min) at different light intensities: (a) 1 sun; (b) 2 sun; (c) 3 sun; (d) 4 sun.
Cycling characteristics test of CQD nanofluid (radiation duration of 24 min) at 1 sun illumination intensity.
(a) Transmission spectral curves of different composite fluids; (b) Temperature variation curves of different composite fluids at 1 sun illumination intensity.
Solar-thermal conversion efficiencies of different working fluids at 1 sun illumination intensity: (a) PEG-200; (b) H2O; (c) PEG-200/CQD-24; (d) H2O/CQD-24; (e) Au/CQD-24.

head.ce:title: Ultra-stable carbon quantum dot nanofluids for direct absorption solar collectors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy Science and Engineering, Central South University, 410083, Changsha, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Energy Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Energy Science and Engineering, Central South University, 410083, Changsha, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanofluids show great interest in the direct absorption solar collector (DASC) due to their excellent optical, thermal, and flow properties. However, the stability of nanofluid in the terms of the long period and the working temperature has limited its commercial application in DASCs. In this work, ultra-stable carbon quantum dot (CQD) nanofluids were prepared by microwave heating polyethylene glycol 200 (PEG-200), which shows excellent stability over a long period (such as 30 days) and repeatable performance at the working temperature. The solar thermal conversion efficiency η increases with extending the microwave duration t, and η can reach 81% at 1 sun and t = 24 min, which is triple the base fluid PEG-200 (η = 27%). In addition, the solar intensity and working temperature have little effect on η due to the ultra-stability of CQD nanofluids. Finally, η can be further enhanced by changing the base fluid or mixing with the plasmonic nanofluids. These results show that the CQD nanofluids can be a potential working fluid used in solar thermal applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Energy and the environment are two main topics in modern social development. Solar energy, as a clean energy source with abundant reserves and promising applications, has received widespread attention in recent years [1]. Solar thermal conversion, as one of the most direct ways to utilize solar radiation, has attracted lots of interest by converting absorbed solar radiation into thermal energy. And then the thermal energy can be further utilized in power generation, thermal catalysis, and seawater desalination [2,3]. Therefore, solar-thermal conversion efficiency is the key for solar thermal applications. Compared with the surface absorption solar collectors, direct absorption solar collectors (DASC) absorb solar radiation directly through the working fluid, which can reduce the temperature difference between the absorber and the environment to increase the solar-thermal conversion efficiency [4,5].

Nanofluids show excellent optical, thermal, and flow properties by dispersing nanoparticles in the base fluid [6,7], which can be used in DASCs to improve the solar-thermal conversion efficiency [8]. Over the years, various nanofluids have been investigated to improve the solar-thermal conversion performance of working fluids in solar collectors. For example, the solar absorption efficiency of the single-walled carbon nanotube nanofluid can be increased by 105% compared with pure water (H2O) at a thickness of 10 cm and a concentration of 1 ppm due to the intrinsic absorption ability of carbon materials [9]. By coupling the plasmonic core and carbon shell, the solar absorption efficiency of the Cu@C core-shell nanofluid can be increased by 388% compared with the pure H2O at a thickness of 0.2 cm and a volume fraction of 20 ppm [10]. In addition, by adding a thin layer of Ti3C2Tx to the base fluid H2O, the solar thermal conversion efficiency can be improved by 31% at a mass fraction of 0.02 wt% [11]. These results indicate that nanofluids can greatly enhance the solar-thermal conversion performance of the working fluids [12,13].

However, the stability of nanofluids has limited their application in solar collectors since the optical absorption performance would be weakened when nanoparticles agglomerate or settle [14]. To improve the stability of the working fluid, some mechanical or chemical methods can be used. For example, mechanical methods (such as ultrasonic vibration and ball milling) can improve stability by reducing the cluster size [15,16]. Chemical methods can enhance stability by electrostatic stabilization (using ionic surfactants, pH adjustment, and covalent surface functionalization [17–19]) and steric stabilization (using non-ionic surfactants, and addition of polymers [20,21]). While these methods still limit the large-scale applications of nanofluids. For example, the mechanical method couldn't maintain stability for a long period while the additives (surfactants or polymers) are usually sensitive to the working temperature and would increase the cost [22,23]. Therefore, it's still significant to develop a simple and rapid method to prepare low-cost, green, and thermally stable nanofluids.

In the past few years, carbon quantum dots (CQD) show great performances in the physicochemical, optical, and electronic properties and low cost, receiving lots of attention in biological applications, catalysis, energy absorption, and conversion [24,25]. In this study, the solar thermal conversion performance of CQD nanofluids was investigated. Firstly, CQD nanofluid was prepared by microwave heating PEG-200, and the optical properties and long-period stability were analyzed. Then, the solar-thermal conversion efficiency and cyclic stability of CQD nanofluids were experimentally studied under simulated solar irradiation, and the solar-thermal conversion efficiency was calculated using the energy conservation model. Effects of microwave duration, solar intensity, and working temperature on the efficiency were also analyzed. Finally, the solar-thermal conversion efficiency of CQD nanofluid was further enhanced by changing the base fluid or mixing plasmonic nanofluids.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbon quantum dot nanofluid
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, CQD nanofluid was synthesized by the microwave heating method. Microwaves in the wavelength range of 1 mm to 1 m would be absorbed by dipole materials (such as H2O), resulting in local heating [26]. PEG is an environmental polymer with non-toxic and good H2O solubility. And PEG has many dipoles, and the interaction with the microwave makes its temperature rise sharply, leading to PEG carbonization [27].

A home-used microwave oven (Galanz-P70F23P-G5) with a power of 700 W was used to microwave 5 mL of PEG-200 (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.) in Fig. 1 a. In the microwave heating process, PEG chains are broken down and oxidized into various fragments in the presence of oxygen. Then the alkenes fragments gather to form a hydrophobic core, and the hydrophilic, oxygen-containing fragments remain more around the cluster surface, forming a surface passivation layer (Fig. 1b) [28]. The microwave duration t can be used to control the carbonization degree (i.e., CQD concentration).

The carbonization temperature after stopping microwave heating was monitored by an infrared thermographic camera (Fluke Ti400+). The morphology and particle size of CQDs were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (FEI-TALOS-F200X). The working fluid was placed in a quartz cuvette with an optical range of 10 mm, and the cuvette was placed on the upper surface of the integrating sphere (IS-30-6-R/IS-30-6-T), making sure that the cuvette completely covers the light entrance of the integrating sphere. The light source was perpendicular to the upper surface of the cuvette and the visible to near-infrared transmission spectra (400 nm–1700 nm) were measured using fiber optic spectrometers from ideaoptics (PG2000 for 400 nm–1000 nm and NIR-1700 for 1000 nm–1700 nm). The specific heat capacities of samples were measured from 20 °C to 80 °C using a differential scanning calorimeter (Swiss-Mettler-Toledo-DSC3) with a temperature rise rate of 5 °C/min.

To determine the solar-thermal conversion efficiency of CQD nanofluids, a simple experiment device was used based on the energy balance equation (Fig. 2 ). And the real experimental setup was shown in Fig. S1. The sample fluid (∼22 mL) was stirred (∼500 rpm) in a quartz square vessel (3 cm х 3 cm х 3 cm) to uniform the temperature distribution. The vessel was kept in the air by a fixture to avoid direct contact with the upper surface of the magnetic stirrer. The top of the vessel was covered with a sponge plate to reduce samples evaporation. Three T-type thermocouples were placed at different distances (0.75, 1.5, and 2.25 cm) along the solar radiation direction to monitor the temperature. The working fluid was heated by simulated sunlight (CEL HXF300, Aulight Inc., China) with different intensities (1, 2, 3, and 4 sun). The solar radiation continued for 60 min to reach the thermal equilibrium state. And then it was turned off, the working fluid sample was cooled to room temperature naturally.

To evaluate the solar-thermal conversion efficiency of nanofluids, two models were usually used. In the first model, the solar-thermal conversion efficiency is calculated based on the sensible heat [29,30]：
(1)
m f
c f
Δ T =
I 0
A i
η Δ t
where m f is the working fluid mass, C f is the working fluid specific heat, in this paper, the specific heat of CQD nanofluid is calculated by the equation fitted in Fig. S2, and the specific heat of the base fluid is obtained by reference to the literature [31–33], ΔT is the temperature difference between the t moment and the starting time, Δt is the solar radiation time, I 0 is the solar radiation intensity, A i is the irradiated area, and η is the solar-thermal conversion efficiency. In this model, the solar-thermal conversion efficiency is the ratio of the absorbed sensible heat by the working fluid to the total solar radiation, without considering the heat dissipation to the environment. Hence, the result is more accurate when the working fluid and the ambient temperatures are close and the heat loss is low. As the working fluid temperature increases, the increase in heat loss to the environment, resulting in a decrease in solar-thermal conversion efficiency.

The second model is based on the energy conservation equation, taking into account the heat loss to the surrounding environment [34,35]:
(2)
m f
c f
d T
d t
+
Q l
=
I 0
A i
η
where Q l is the heat loss of the working fluid to the surrounding environment, which is mainly determined by thermal convection and is related to the working temperature. While the convection heat transfer coefficient is constant and can be determined by the cooling process after turning off the solar simulator at the same conditions as the heating process.

Based on the above two models, the solar-thermal conversion efficiency can be divided into two parts, which are solar heating efficiency and cooling efficiency as follows [36,37]:
(3)
η h
( T )
=
m f
c f
I 0
A i
d T
d t
(4)
η c
( T )
= −
m f
c f
I 0
A i
d T
d t
where η h is the solar heating efficiency, calculated by the sensible heat of the working fluid in the solar heating process, η c is the cooling efficiency, calculated by the sensible heat of the working fluid in the cooling process after turning off the solar simulator. Hence, the sum of η h and η c is the solar-thermal conversion efficiency at the working temperature T:
(5)
η
( T )
=
η h
( T )
+
η c
( T )

After microwave radiation, a local heating area can be found in PEG-200 (Fig. 3 a). With increasing the radiation duration t from 3 min to 24 min (interval is 3 min), the local temperature increases from 241 °C to 294 °C. (Fig. 3b). And the larger temperature leads to a higher carbonization degree, resulting in a darker color of the fluid (Fig. 3c). When the radiation time exceeds 24 min, the stable CQD nanofluid could not be formed due to the high local temperature and excessive carbonization. The sample pictures at the radiation time of 27 min are shown in Fig. S3. TEM characterization of the CQD nanofluid with a microwave radiation time of 24 min (Fig. 3d) shows that the size of CQDs is smaller than 10 nm, which is consistent with the results of other researchers [38,39], indicating that CQDs can be stably dispersed in the base fluid. On the other hand, it can be found that the transmittance of CQD nanofluids significantly decreases with increasing radiation duration t in λ = 400–1400 nm (Fig. 3e). The pure PEG-200 is almost transparent in the visible part while at t = 24 min the carbonized PEG-200 (i.e., CQD nanofluid) is black. And with a thickness of 10 mm, the averaged solar transmittance is only 2.5% at λ = 400–800 nm, indicating that the CQD nanofluid can be a potential working fluid in solar thermal applications.

Besides the optical absorption properties, stability in the long period is also important for the working fluid. It can be seen from the optical images in Fig. 4 a that there is little sediment at the bottom of the vessel after storing it for 30 days. In addition, the transmittance spectra also change little, indicating that CQD nanofluids have great dispersibility and stability over a long period.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solar-thermal conversion performances of CQD nanofluids
ce:section.ce:para: As discussed in Section 2.3, the solar-thermal conversion efficiency is calculated based on the energy balance equation, accounting for the heat dissipation to the environment. Uniform temperature distribution in the fluid is important to calculate the efficiency accurately. It can be found that the temperature would decrease exponentially along with the solar radiation direction (Fig. 5 a), which would make it difficult to calculate the whole energy based on these several temperature points. By introducing the stirring process in the fluid, a uniform temperature distribution can be achieved (Fig. 5b), which can promote the calculation efficiently in Section 2.3.

Fig. 6 a shows the heating and cooling processes of different CQD nanofluids under stirring conditions. It can be found that the temperature increases rapidly after solar radiation, and then reach a steady state due to heat dissipation. Finally, the temperature drops quickly to room temperature after turning off the solar simulator. On the other hand, CQD nanofluid with a long duration time achieves the larger temperature due to the high solar absorption ability based on Fig. 3e. The maximal temperature increase can be 4.7, 5.5, 9.6, and 13.1 °C at microwave duration t = 0, 3, 12, and 24 min respectively.

According to the energy balance, the solar-thermal conversion efficiency η can be determined by summing the solar heating efficiency η h and the cooling efficiency η c (Fig. 6a ∼ 6e). It can be found that η h decreases while η c increases with increasing the working temperature since the convection heat dissipation are proportional to the working temperature, resulting in the total η being independent with the working temperature. Hence, it's of significance to consider the heat dissipation to evaluate the solar-thermal conversion performance of nanofluids. Based on the heating and cooling processes, η can be 27%, 37%, 66%, and 81% at the microwave duration time of 0, 3, 12, and 24 min respectively. After microwave carbonization for 24 min, η is increased by 200% compared with the PEG-200.

For the effect of solar intensity on the solar-thermal conversion performance, different solar intensities (1, 2, 3, and 4 sun) are conducted in Fig. 7 . Increasing the solar intensity can greatly enhance the temperature rise of the working fluid. For example, the temperature increases from 13.1 °C to 39.2 °C when solar intensity increases from 1 sun to 4 sun. Since the efficiency calculation has considered heat dissipation, the total η is also independent of the solar intensity (i.e., working temperature), which is ∼81% at different solar intensities. While for the heating and cooling efficiencies, both absolute values of the slope decrease with increasing the solar intensity since the higher solar intensity would lead to larger working temperature and heat dissipation.

As discussed above, the solar-thermal conversion efficiency is independent of the working temperature experimentally, indicating the CQD nanofluid has great stability over the working temperature. To further confirm the stability of CQD nanofluids, the cycling stability of CQD nanofluids is conducted in Fig. 8 . It can be found that the temperature rises under five cycles change little, which are ∼13 °C. These results indicate that CQD nanofluid prepared by microwave heating has great stability over a long period and working temperatures, can be a potential working fluid for solar thermal applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced solar-thermal conversion performances of CQD nanofluids
ce:section.ce:para: As discussed above, CQD nanofluid has great absorption performance in the visible part due to the intrinsic absorption of carbon materials. While the near-infrared absorption ability is limited, which can be enhanced by mixing different fluids with CQDs.

For the base fluid H2O and PEG-200, it can be found that both of them are highly transparent in the visible part while H2O has a stronger absorption ability in the near-infrared region than that of PEG-200 (Fig. 9 a). Hence, by mixing H2O with the CQD nanofluids in a volume ratio of 1:1, the absorption ability in the near-infrared region can be enhanced due to H2O absorption while the absorption ability in the visible part is similar (H2O/CQD-24 vs. PEG-200/CQD-24). To further improve the absorption performance of the working fluid, Au nanofluid with a volume fraction of 2.5 ppm is mixed with CQD-24 nanofluid in a volume ratio of 1:1 to form an Au/CQD-24 nanofluid. It can be found that the absorption ability in
λ
= 800–1200 nm is enhanced compared with the H2O/CQD-24 nanofluid. Hence, under solar radiation, the Au/CQD-24 nanofluid has the largest temperature rise (∼10 °C). While the temperature rise of H2O, PEG-200, H2O/CQD-24, and Au/CQD-24 nanofluids is 4.5 °C, 4.8 °C, 9.3 °C, and 9.7 °C, respectively.

Based on the above fluids in Fig. 9, the solar-thermal conversion efficiencies are calculated for the heating and cooling processes at a solar intensity of 1 sun (Fig. 10 ). It can be found that the base fluid PEG-200 achieves η = 27% while the η of H2O is 31% due to the high absorption ability in the near-infrared region. Adding H2O into the CQD-24 nanofluids makes η increase from 65% (PEG-200/CQD-24) to 75% (H2O/CAD-24). η of the Au/CQD-24 nanofluid reaches ∼81%, which is 25% higher than PEG-200/CQD-24 and 200% higher than PEG-200. In addition, the temperature rise and solar-thermal conversion performance at a larger solar intensity of 4 sun are also conducted in Fig. S4 of the supporting information. These results indicate that CQD nanofluids with great stability and solar-thermal conversion performance can be used in solar thermal applications.

In this work, ultra-stable CQD nanofluid was prepared by microwave heating and an energy balance model was used to evaluate the solar thermal conversion performance of nanofluids with a consideration of heat dissipation. Increasing the microwave radiation duration can enhance the carbonization degree of PEG-200, resulting in a larger solar-thermal efficiency. And the solar-thermal efficiency of CQD-24 nanofluid can be ∼81%, which is three times the base fluid PEG-200. In addition, mixing different fluids (such as H2O or plasmonic nanofluids) can further enhance the solar thermal efficiency of CQD nanofluids. Results also show that the solar-thermal conversion efficiency is independent of the working temperature and solar intensity, indicating the great stability of CQD nanofluids. In addition, the cycle heating and cooling experiments also indicate the CQD nanofluid can maintain its performance. CQD nanofluids with great stability and optical absorption performance can be a potential working fluid for direct absorption solar collectors.

Xingyu Chen: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Software, Methodology. Zhekun Xiong: Software, Data curation. Meijie Chen: Supervision, Methodology, Conceptualization. Ping Zhou: Validation, Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Central South University (Grant No. 2021zzts0156), the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province (Grant No. 2021JJ40732), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 52006246).
